item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The first image that was used in this article comes from here and is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0). Thus, it has been removed. The second image was created for the article and thus is cc-compliant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Downvote. You don't need like 6 or 8 collapsibles that each one have a paragraph.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This just doesn't cut it, too bogged down by repetition and useless details, too much stuff. And the 100% reflective mirror stuff can not exist in reality. I suggest a rewrite.

Downvoted

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This looked like your basic Slenderman expy for a really long time. And then I hit the last addendum, and everything changed.

The explanation in the addendum is an excellent concept for an article, and "I thought it was X until the end" is a format that totally works, but this one could use some improvement.

+ Recommendations
- OK

I would recommend cutting the first two test logs- they're a big chunk of text that doesn't tell us anything we don't already know from the description, which is generally a turn-off. You could add a summary to the beginning of the last log to let us know that D-10199 has been infected with 2216-1 and has shown the capability to transmit it. (Maybe even note an excerpt from D-10199's description of 2216-1, indicating that their personal 2216-1 resembles them physically in some minor way.)

I really like the note about 4/2216 clearance, especially as an indicator of how virulent and dangerous the Foundation considers the phenomenon. (This and the description of how 2216-1 transmits both become hilariously off-base responses once you read the last addendum, too, which I consider a bonus. If I'm interpreting Xyank's theory correctly, SCP-2216-1 doesn't transmit itself from video to video at all, let alone through the human brain…)

Procedure 851-Bellerophon is well-written, and I like how it implies the Foundation has rigorously observed 2216-3 manifestations and responded by creating a deliberately "inviting" testing facility… but in hindsight, it doesn't add anything to our understanding of the phenomenon, it takes up lots of space and text, and most importantly, it doesn't make any sense for it to be there once you've read the last addendum. I understand that Bellerophon made sense when it was conceived, but now it's just a set of instructions for how to accelerate a possible end of the world. It's gone from "useful" to "useless AND dangerous;" the Foundation would be much better off getting rid of it entirely. (There are some clever bits, like the use of photographs on the walls to occupy the subject's attention so that 2216-3 can appear in their peripheral vision, but again, those could be conveyed entirely from the test log without losing anything.)

I also enjoyed the supplemental items whose text has been removed- they don't describe anything, but they show you that a) the Foundation changes things sometimes, which feels real, and b) Document 2216-X1999-1 is important. This is good, because that's a damn excellent document. They also do this while taking a minimum of the reader's time, which is important.

One question about the anomaly:

Originally I thought SCP-2216-1 had a single identity, or a fixed number of instances- it was one person in all the videos and they looked different because they were wearing different clothes or viewed from different angles, or it was one nonhuman entity using different human appearances. That fit with the idea of it being transmitted from one video to another, and with all the Foundation's subjects being infected from the same source- if you watch a video with an entity in it, you see the entity; if you see the entity, you (maybe) get "infected" and can spread that entity to other videos.

After the addendum, it becomes clear that each "infected" viewer has their own 2216-1. A viewer "infecting" a previously unaffected video still makes sense- that person's future self knows which videos they're going to watch, and can use whatever temporal phenomenon is going on to appear where and when those videos were recorded, so that their past self sees them on the screen. But now the idea of a video infecting a person is more complicated.

Does a viewer get infected by the sight of their own 2216-1, or will anyone's be sufficient? If they see their own 2216-1 in the first 2216-2 that they watch, is it because that image was always there, or because the video changed to include it (in the same way that other videos will change later on)?

I'd expect the Foundation to come up with this idea and investigate it, because there's at least one easy test: ask each subject to count the people who appear in the video that infects them, and then compare counts. If the count changes substantially after a number of new viewers, the video is probably showing new images, which tells us that uninfected viewers affect their initial video (and thus there's something special about them even before they start watching). If the count doesn't change, I'd expect that to be noted instead, if only to rule out the possibility- "Reports of the contents of an existing SCP-2216-2 instance have been consistent over time, with subjects identifying SCP-2216-1's appearance in the same place at the same timestamp; this confirms that the SCP-2216-3 phenomenon is initiated by the video rather than the individual viewer." or similar.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not finished? then i wont vote for now

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
i like the concept, but the containment procedures are a little bit weird
neutral vote for now…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I barely understood any of the concept or ideas, and that's not an inherently bad thing for a skip (everyone will understand skips differently) but from what I could tell, there was nothing consistent about it. So everyone who is aware of this will develop symptoms and die, except if they get amnestics? But only certain people are barely affected? And the extraneous link between this and 1999, from what I could tell, seems tacked-on. I feel like I'm talking about my ass because I can't understand any of this, but when it doesn't appear to have consistency in any of it, I just get lost and give up frustrated.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's long, there's not that great of a payoff and I downvoted when I read "100% reflective one-way lenses". If it's 100% reflective, no light would get through and you would see nothing from the other side of the glass.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Faraday cages are still effective when the door's open except for a cone with line of sight through the door. You'd probably want to make the wall facing the cage door transmission-insulated just to be certain, but really so long as the field stays intact you're golden. Remember, faraday cages are made of mesh; so long as the uniform charge field isn't compromised, they can handle having a hole opened in them.

Seconded on the mirrored glasses. Polarized glass, on the other hand, might be able to filter out the frequency(s), or at least enough to scramble it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1) If SCP-2216-1 only affects people who are aware that it's anomalous, how did anyone become aware in the first place that any of them were anomalous?

2) How do researchers or MTF members tell the difference between SCP-2216-1 instances and non-anomalous recordings of people with their identifying features covered?

Five (5) instances of SCP-2216-2 are to be kept at Site-19/2216 in separate storage lockers equipped with a Faraday cage to prevent wireless transmission of SCP-2216-1.

The transmissions would be let through again as soon as the cage was open. They'd need to be stored in a room/vault which was shielded, which also had a shielded "airlock" to prevent any signal from leaking, and they'd have to be placed in a shielded box for transport before leaving the room/vault.

All MTF Iota-7 agents and level 4/2216 researchers are to wear one-way mirrored, 100% reflective eye protection when viewing any visual media or subjects who have viewed an instance of SCP-2216-2.

A 100% reflective one way mirror wouldn't let any light through.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the supplement but it's left me with some difficulty groking the effect here in the main article. Is the entity viewers see on the tape the same as they see in person? Are one or both of them Elfie, or is Xyank right in at least one case?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: thanks. sorry about that

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is such a very long article, for so very little payoff. It essentially amounts to "Spooky time people kill you if you watch them for too long."

It started off promisingly, but I started saying to myself "Okay, I get it" over and over, as the wide range of confusing logs and notes were either redundant or conflicting, to the point in which I lost most of my interest. By the time that I got to the end, there was nothing there to keep interest. The ending consists of two pictures that simply validates something that you already said earlier.

I was expecting the article to go out with a bang, but this one just kind of fizzled out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Keter-class items don't have to be specifically hostile toward humans. They don't even have to be sapient. There really isn't a strict set of criteria, but in my time here I've seen that they generally should fail the locked box test and have very significant consequences (not necessarily "everybody dies." It could even be something like "everybody forgets how clocks work.") They can also be poorly understood, making it difficult to be certain whether it is being fully contained. But that's just my understanding. Others may interpret it differently.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Please do not add nonexistent tags, such as "video" and "entity". Leave that to staff, if you don't know how.

For more information, read the Tag Guide.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
suggestions welcome.

I completely agree with you. when I first put it up for criticism, that was the first comment, that it was so long it was ineffective. I'm just not sure how to make it shorter while maintaining the core concept.

I think I can remove the video descriptions, and describe the applicable ones in the test logs. but otherwise I'm not sure how to handle this.

edit: take a look now, I think I streamlined it a little bit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Normally, I'm all about being thorough in my skips, often to the criticism of my peers. This, however, feels bogged down. I like this. However, I feel like it needs to be pruned a great deal to be effective. I found myself wanting to skim. This needs more punch.

item.title: Supplemental stuff
item.content:encoded: The last document is open. take a gander if you like.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: that's fair, I had just already written everything with the 2216 designation and didn't want to wait to change it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, that wasn't a good idea. Can't post an incomplete article and expect it to have the full effect.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That was kind of the point of the image; enough to be something but not enough to be descript. The final note is going to lead to a document I haven't finished yet but I wanted to post this article before the 2216 number was taken. silly reason I guess, but hindsight is 20/20

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The final note still being redacted was a bit of a letdown. This is a nicely creepy setup, at least, but nothing really comes of it. (Can't figure out what's in that second camera image, either.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I always understood that safe was something that was reliably contained if procedure was followed, keter was something aggressive that actively attempted to breach containment, and euclid was everything else. I didn't want to give the impression of SCP-2216-1 as actively hostile toward humanity.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I chose to introduce it, but list it's anomalous effects after describing SCP-2216-2. It seemed to flow better to not say, "here's the entity and here's the scary thing it does, it's found on these tapes" just seemed better to describe it briefly, then where it's found, then what it does. idk, maybe differences of opinion, any suggestions?

edit: i looked back and made some changes. I just don't see anyway to accurately describe the creature and effects without the length that it's at. any suggestions are welcome.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the idea, but the text is thick and not very entertaining to read. It felt like pushing through molasses to get to the matter. An edit for conciseness and clarity would be in order.

Also, if it's as prevalent, dangerous, and uncontainable as I'm getting the impression - with those double digit percentile redactions - it stands a chance for reclassification to keter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Concept is solid, but the pacing really broke my immersion. A handful of lines in the Description ought to be in the containment procedures and I believe you should list the effects of viewing the entity directly after describing it, instead of writing it in at a seemingly random spot.

item.title: Re: Secondary title
item.content:encoded: thanks

item.title: Re: Secondary title
item.content:encoded: You need to edit the actual List. Remove the [ACCESS DENIED] and type in your title.

item.title: Secondary title
item.content:encoded: Hey guys, I could figure out how to get the secondary title? all articles are "SCP-XXXX - title here" on the listing page, can someone show me how to add the "title here" part?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Part of that is the fact that I'm not done with the additional documents. I'll be including them as separate pages but I haven't finished the writing. For now I thought the core concept was okay, and I didn't really want to wait and risk having to change all the SCP-XXXX numbers.

I really couldn't find a way to write technically as a scientific article would be written and include all the detail to get the point across. any ideas on shortening it up without sacrificing the writing style?

Edit: I fixed some things. I'm going to get the supplemental pages up tomorrow so by then it should be fully complete.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The concept of fucking up reality by punching holes where our self met our other self sounds fucking awesome.

The execution, however, feels muffled under the weight of testing logs and unnecessary notes that turn out to be redacted anyways.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A small thing I have to point out:

Researchers who have completed work with SCP-2216 are to be debriefed and administered Class-C amnesiacs before leaving the premises of Site-19/2216.

'Amnesiac' is supposed to be either amnestic or amnesic. Slipped me up the first couple of times as well.
